## [00:00 - 03:04]

Hi, this is Don Crather. Let's talk about live and recorded testimonials in your webinar. So as we've mentioned ad nauseam up to this point, results and case studies and testimonials can be incredibly powerful. In fact, they can be the core of your success in your webinar. And it's one thing to have text versions of them. So to have maybe a picture of that person and then what they said about what it is that you do. It's even better to literally have that testimonial or that case study in their own words, not just in text, but literally in their own words that they can listen to and maybe even watch. So there are two ways to do this. One way is to is to play a recording of them talking about their case study or their testimonial. The second one is to actually have them live on the webinar to share their story. So playing a recording. You can get them to record things. Make sure your webinar platform does that well. Not all webinar platforms are designed in in every aspect because it takes a lot of bandwidth. It takes a lot of processing on their end to be able to play a recording. And so many of them are not designed to play recordings. But some of them are make sure yours is one of those before you decide to do this and then make sure you test it like crazy so you get it absolutely right. Your platform may have the ability to do it, but not in the version that most people are using. So for example, zoom dot us has a certain setting that if you use that the video will play nice and smoothly. If not, it'll buffer and have all kinds of breaks and everything else like that. And so just realize you probably want to call your tech support person from the from the webinar company and ask them this question and say what's the best way to do this. They probably know that answer and can help you to do it if their program allows that. Then there's the concept of live presentations where you actually bring them on live. This can be very conversational because then your it's a conversation between the two of you, not just you pressing play and having them talk. And it also allows your audience to ask them questions to clarify, which can help to support the testimonial that they're giving you. And oftentimes you get a side benefit that they'll stick around in chat and answer people's questions, even without you asking them, though I do suggest you ask them. Now the risk is you're not in control. They could go. I one time was on a webinar. I wasn't holding this one, but I was on a webinar where they brought somebody in and they were expecting them to spend about two minutes and they spent 20 is like, well, there went that. And so I usually do a call with

them before they so that I can outline expectations and everyone understands exactly what they're going to do, what they're not going to do. And I actually have them rehearse it. So I do recommend if you can actually having people live to give testimonials on your webinar, it can make a huge amount of difference in the credibility and believability of all the testimonials you've done throughout your entire webinar.
